items.title: Understanding the Etiologic Agent of Relapsing Fever
items.content: Relapsing fever is caused by Borrelia spirochetes, which are characterized by their unique morphology and limited biosynthetic capacity. The infection's relapsing nature is due to antigenic variation in surface proteins.
keyPoints: Caused by Borrelia spirochetes
Spirochetes have a genome content one-quarter that of Escherichia coli
Antigenic variation leads to the relapsing course of infection
Borrelia cells are 10-20 µm long and 0.2–0.3 µm in diameter
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · Relapsing Fever | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Relapsing Fever
items.content: Louse-borne relapsing fever (LBRF) presents with two or more episodes of fever separated by periods of well-being. Diagnosis is confirmed by identifying large numbers of Borrelia spirochetes in the blood.
keyPoints: Characterized by multiple fever episodes
Diagnosis involves detecting spirochetes in the blood
LBRF was one of the first diseases linked to a specific microbe
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · Relapsing Fever | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: Transmission Dynamics of LBRF and STRF
items.content: LBRF is transmitted by body lice, with humans as the sole reservoir, while STRF involves soft ticks from the genus Ornithodoros. STRF has a broader geographic distribution due to its association with various mammalian hosts.
keyPoints: Borrelia recurrentis (LBRF) is acquired from body louse bites and not directly through the bite itself.
Humans are the sole reservoir for LBRF, which can occur in conditions like famine or war.
STRF is transmitted by soft ticks of the genus Ornithodoros, with geographic distribution excluding arctic environments.
Most STRF Borrelia species use small to medium-sized mammals as reservoirs, except Borrelia duttonii, which involves human-to-human transmission in sub-Saharan Africa.

items.title: Borrelia miyamotoi Transmission and Prevalence
items.content: B. miyamotoi is transmitted by Ixodes hard ticks, similar to those transmitting Lyme disease, with transmission occurring early in the tick's feeding process.
keyPoints: Borrelia miyamotoi is transmitted through outdoor activities involving contact with Ixodes hard ticks.
Prevalence of antibodies to B. miyamotoi is about one-fourth that of Borrelia burgdorferi in areas where both coexist.
Transmission can occur from tick larvae, nymphs, and adults, unlike B. burgdorferi.

items.title: Mechanism of Relapsing Fever Pathogenesis and Immune Response
items.content: Relapsing fever spirochetes, such as STRF and HTRF, enter the host through tick saliva. STRF proliferates rapidly in blood with a doubling time of 6 hours, reaching concentrations from $10^{-6}$ to $10^{7}$/mL or more. The pathogenesis involves erythrocyte aggregation leading to hepatosplenomegaly and anemia, and potential bleeding disorders due to thrombocytopenia and vessel blockage. Neurotropic species like B. turicatae can invade the brain, evading host immunity. Borrelia activates Toll-like receptors via lipoproteins, causing a proinflammatory response similar to endotoxemia.
keyPoints: STRF spirochetes double every 6 hours, reaching $10^{-6}-10^{7}$/mL in blood.
Erythrocyte aggregation leads to hepatosplenomegaly and anemia.
Bleeding disorders may result from thrombocytopenia and vessel blockage.
Neurotropic species like B. turicatae can invade the brain.
Lipoproteins activate Toll-like receptors, causing a proinflammatory response.

items.title: Immune Response and Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction in Relapsing Fever
items.content: IgM antibodies specific to Borrelia surface lipoproteins appear after a few days, leading to bacterial lysis via direct action or opsonization. This triggers a 'crisis' with symptoms like increased temperature and hypotension due to the release of bacterial products. A similar acute worsening post-antibiotic treatment is known as the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (JHR), characterized by an abrupt decline in patient condition.
keyPoints: IgM antibodies appear after a few days, causing bacterial lysis.
'Crisis' symptoms include increased temperature and hypotension.
Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction occurs post-antibiotic treatment with acute worsening.

items.title: Fever Pattern and Crisis in Relapsing Fever
items.content: Relapsing fever, including both STRF and LBRF, is characterized by sudden onset of high fevers (≥39°C) that are punctuated by afebrile periods. In STRF, the pattern includes at least two febrile episodes with intervening afebrile periods lasting a few days. The first fever episode in STRF often ends in a crisis phase marked by rigors and further elevation in temperature, followed by diaphoresis, falling temperature, and hypotension.
keyPoints: Temperature during fevers can reach as high as 43°C
First fever episode in STRF may end with a crisis lasting ~15–30 min
Crisis phase includes rigors, increased pulse and blood pressure, followed by diaphoresis and hypotension

items.title: Neurologic Manifestations in Relapsing Fever
items.content: Relapsing fever can present with neurologic symptoms. STRF is more likely to have localizing neurologic findings compared to LBRF or HTRF. In North America, B. turicatae infection often leads to aseptic meningitis and cranial neuritis, including Bell's palsy, typically presenting in the second or third febrile episode.
keyPoints: STRF has more common localizing neurologic findings than LBRF
B. turicatae can cause aseptic meningitis and cranial neuritis
Bell's palsy often presents in the second or third febrile episode

items.title: Cardiac Involvement in Relapsing Fever
items.content: Myocarditis is a potential complication of relapsing fever, evidenced by gallops on cardiac auscultation, prolonged QTc interval, and cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema visible on chest radiography.
keyPoints: Gallops may be heard on cardiac auscultation
Prolonged QTc interval is indicative of myocarditis
Cardiomegaly and pulmonary edema can be seen on chest radiography

items.title: Recognizing Relapsing Fever: Epidemiology and Timing for Blood Smear Collection
items.content: Relapsing fever, caused by Borrelia species such as STRF or LBRF, should be suspected in patients with a characteristic fever pattern and recent exposure to body lice or soft ticks. Diagnosis can occur years after the last local case due to tick longevity and transovarial transmission. Blood smears are most effective when collected at or just before the peak of fever.
keyPoints: Consider STRF or LBRF in patients with characteristic fever patterns and recent exposure to body lice or soft ticks.
Diagnosis can occur many years after the last local case due to tick longevity and transovarial transmission.
Blood smears are most effective when collected at or just before the peak of fever.
Spirochetes concentration ≥10⁵/mL is needed for detection in thin blood smears.

items.title: Laboratory Diagnosis: Techniques and Sensitivity
items.content: The gold standard for diagnosing LBRF and STRF involves direct spirochete detection via microscopy. PCR is preferred for detecting B. miyamotoi or B. lonestari due to its higher sensitivity, especially between febrile episodes. Serologic tests are limited by cross-reactivity and may not distinguish between Borrelia species.
keyPoints: Direct spirochete detection via microscopy remains the gold standard for LBRF and STRF diagnosis.
PCR is preferred for detecting B. miyamotoi or B. lonestari due to higher sensitivity, especially between febrile episodes.
Serologic tests may be misleading due to cross-reactivity among spirochetes.

items.title: Identifying Key Differential Diagnoses for STRF
items.content: When diagnosing Spotted Tick-Borne Relapsing Fever (STRF), consider infections with periodic fever patterns or extended febrile periods, especially in patients with relevant exposure histories. Important differentials include Rocky Mountain spotted fever, ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis, tick-borne viral infection, and babesiosis in North America, Europe, Russia, and northeastern Asia. In areas where Lyme disease is endemic, STRF presenting with Bell's palsy may be misdiagnosed as Lyme disease.
keyPoints: Consider rickettsioses, ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis, tick-borne viral infections, babesiosis in specific regions
STRF with Bell's palsy can mimic Lyme disease
In other regions, consider malaria, typhoid fever, dengue, and leptospirosis
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Relapsing Fever

items.title: Treatment Strategies for STRF in Resource-Limited Settings
items.content: In resource-limited settings or during epidemics, a single dose of antibiotic can effectively treat LBRF and STRF. Recommended doses include oral doxycycline (200 mg) or tetracycline (500 mg) for adults, and intramuscular ceftriaxone 250 mg for adults and 125 mg for children. For children, dosages are adjusted to 12.5 mg/kg for tetracycline, 5 mg/kg for doxycycline, and 200,000–400,000 units of penicillin G procaine.
keyPoints: Single dose treatment is effective in limited resources
Adult doses: oral doxycycline (200 mg), tetracycline (500 mg), ceftriaxone 250 mg IM
Child doses: tetracycline 12.5 mg/kg, doxycycline 5 mg/kg, penicillin G procaine 200,000–400,000 units
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Relapsing Fever

items.question: Which genus is responsible for causing relapsing fever?
items.explanation: Relapsing fever is caused by infection with any of several species of Borrelia spirochetes.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · Relapsing Fever | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: What characteristic laboratory finding was associated with louse-borne relapsing fever in the nineteenth century?
options: Presence of large numbers of spirochetes in the blood
items.explanation: The presence of large numbers of spirochetes of the genus Borrelia in the blood was a characteristic laboratory finding.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · Relapsing Fever | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: What accounts for the relapsing course of infection with Borrelia spirochetes?
items.explanation: Antigenic variation of the spirochetes' surface proteins accounts for the infection's relapsing course.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · Relapsing Fever | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: Which Borrelia species is primarily maintained through tick transmission between human hosts in sub-Saharan Africa?
items.explanation: Borrelia duttonii is largely maintained by tick transmission between human hosts in sub-Saharan Africa.

items.question: What type of ticks are responsible for transmitting the several species of Borrelia that cause STRF?
options: Soft ticks of the genus Ornithodoros
items.explanation: The several species of Borrelia that cause STRF are transmitted by soft ticks of the genus Ornithodoros.

items.question: In which regions does Borrelia turicatae primarily occur in North America?
items.explanation: Borrelia turicatae occurs in arid southwestern and south-central regions of North America.

items.question: What is the primary consequence of spirochetes binding to erythrocytes in relapsing fever?
items.explanation: Binding of spirochetes to erythrocytes leads to aggregation, sequestration in spleen and liver, causing hepatosplenomegaly and anemia.

items.question: Which immune response is responsible for the lysis of bacteria during relapsing fever?
options: Activation of Toll-like receptors by lipoproteins
Production of IgM antibodies specific to surface lipoprotein
Elevation of interleukin 6 and interleukin 8 concentrations
items.explanation: IgM antibodies specific for the serotype-defining surface lipoprotein cause lysis of bacteria through direct bactericidal action or opsonization.

items.question: What is a Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (JHR) in relapsing fever?
options: A proinflammatory process similar to endotoxemia
An increase in temperature and hypotension after bacterial lysis
Neurotropic infection of the brain by spirochetes
Crossing of the maternal-fetal barrier causing placental damage
items.explanation: JHR is characterized by an abrupt worsening of the patient's condition, including increased temperature and hypotension, after bacterial lysis.

items.question: Which of the following is a common feature during the crisis phase of the first fever episode in STRF?
options: Rigors, further elevation in temperature, increases in pulse and blood pressure
items.explanation: The crisis phase of the first fever episode in STRF includes rigors, a further elevation in temperature, and increases in pulse and blood pressure.

items.question: Which symptom is more likely to be observed during LBRF compared to STRF?
options: Neurologic manifestations such as altered mental state
Multiple febrile periods lasting 1–3 days each
items.explanation: Jaundice, epistaxis, and sub-conjunctival hemorrhages are common during LBRF but not in STRF.

items.question: What is a typical presentation of B. miyamotoi disease?
options: Acute onset of fever with chills, headache, and myalgias starting 1–2 weeks after a tick bite
Recurrent febrile episodes lasting 3–6 days each
Localized skin rash known as erythema migrans
items.explanation: B. miyamotoi disease typically presents with acute onset of fever, chills, headache, and myalgias starting 1–2 weeks after a tick bite.

items.question: Which laboratory finding is common in all forms of relapsing fever?
items.explanation: Mild to moderate normocytic anemia is common in all forms of relapsing fever.

items.question: Which staining method is typically used to reveal spirochetes in thin blood smears for diagnosing LBRF or STRF?
items.explanation: Manual differential counts of white blood cells by Wright, Giemsa, or Giemsa-Wright stain usually reveal spirochetes in thin blood smears.

items.question: When is the preferred time to obtain a blood specimen for diagnosing LBRF and STRF?
items.explanation: The preferred time to obtain a blood specimen is at or just before fever's peak.

items.question: Which diagnostic method is more sensitive than thin blood smear for detecting Borrelia species between febrile episodes?
items.explanation: Overall, NAAT is more sensitive than thin blood smear, particularly for samples obtained between febrile episodes.

items.question: Which of the following infections might be considered in the differential diagnosis for STRF due to similar fever patterns?
options: Rocky Mountain spotted fever and other rickettsioses
items.explanation: Rocky Mountain spotted fever and other rickettsioses can feature periodicity in the fever pattern similar to STRF.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Relapsing Fever

items.question: In areas endemic for both Lyme disease and STRF with Bell's palsy, which condition might be misdiagnosed as the other?
options: STRF may be misdiagnosed as Lyme disease
Lyme disease may be misdiagnosed as STRF
Neither condition is related to Bell's palsy
items.explanation: STRF with Bell's palsy may be misdiagnosed as Lyme disease in areas where both diseases are endemic.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Relapsing Fever

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically considered in the differential diagnosis for STRF outside North America, Europe, Russia, and northeastern Asia?
items.explanation: Influenza is not typically considered in the differential diagnosis for STRF outside North America, Europe, Russia, and northeastern Asia.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Relapsing Fever

items.statement: The majority of current cases of relapsing fever are louse-borne.
items.explanation: Most cases of relapsing fever are now tick-borne, not louse-borne.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · Relapsing Fever | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.statement: Borrelia cells have a genome content one-quarter that of Escherichia coli.
items.explanation: The limited biosynthetic capacity of Borrelia is accounted for by its smaller genome compared to E. coli.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · Relapsing Fever | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.statement: LBRF transmission is currently limited to Ethiopia, Eritrea, and Somalia.
items.explanation: The passage states that LBRF transmission is currently limited to these regions.

items.statement: Borrelia miyamotoi can only be transmitted by nymphs and adult ticks.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that B. miyamotoi may also be acquired from tick larvae, not just nymphs and adults.
Borrelia species are extracellular pathogens; their presence inside cells indicates dead bacteria after phagocytosis.

items.statement: The Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction occurs due to the release of bacterial products from dying spirochetes.
items.explanation: Release of lipoproteins and other bacterial products from dying bacteria provokes a crisis, leading to JHR.

items.statement: In LBRF, the first episode of fever usually ends with a single milder febrile episode.
items.explanation: The first episode of fever in LBRF is unremitting for 3–6 days and is usually followed by a single milder episode.

items.statement: Neurologic manifestations such as altered mental state in LBRF are due to direct invasion of the nervous system.
items.explanation: In LBRF, neurologic manifestations like altered mental state are thought to be secondary to systemic inflammation or small hemorrhages rather than direct invasion.

items.statement: A positive IgG immunoassay of a single specimen always indicates the present illness.
items.explanation: A positive IgG immunoassay may be due to past infection and not necessarily the current illness.

items.statement: PCR is the preferred procedure for direct detection of B. miyamotoi or B. lonestari in blood or CSF.
items.explanation: PCR is indeed the preferred method for detecting these Borrelia species.

items.statement: Spirochetes are no longer detectable in the blood within a few hours after the first dose of an effective antibiotic.
items.explanation: Effective antibiotics can clear spirochetes from the bloodstream within a few hours.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Relapsing Fever

items.statement: Tetracyclines are recommended for use in pregnant and nursing women.
items.explanation: Tetracyclines are contraindicated in pregnant and nursing women.
items.reference: Relapsing Fever · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Relapsing Fever

items.assertion: Unilateral or bilateral Bell's palsy is the most common form of cranial neuritis in STRF.
items.reason: It typically presents in the second or third febrile episode, not the first.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Bell's palsy is most common in later episodes.

items.assertion: Myocarditis in STRF or LBRF can be evidenced by a prolonged QTc interval.
items.reason: This finding is due to elevated serum concentrations of aminotransferases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the prolonged QTc interval is not caused by elevated aminotransferase levels.
